conversation.Experiencer: I was unable to attend my son's concert because of work commitments. I keep going over it in my head, wondering if there was something I could’ve done differently, but it feels like everything was locked in place before I even had a chance to try.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it's been weighing on you a lot. Want to share more about how you're feeling about it?

conversation.Experiencer: It just feels like I should've found a way, you know? Even though I knew the schedule wasn’t flexible, I keep questioning if I missed some opportunity to make it work.

conversation.Experiencer: I think it’s the fact that I knew what I was agreeing to when I took this job, but when it came down to it, facing the actual impact of those decisions hit harder than I expected.

conversation.Responder: That realization sounds tough—it’s like it all became real in the moment. What’s been coming up for you since then?

conversation.Experiencer: Just the chance for them to come out on top—it felt like things were turning around at one point, but it didn’t last.

conversation.Experiencer: They took some risks that didn’t pay off, and from there, it just felt like the momentum completely shifted against them.

conversation.Experiencer: It’s been a week, and I still can’t help but think about how she smiled at me—it was so simple, yet it felt like it lit up the whole moment.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like that smile really stuck with you—it must've felt special in the moment.

conversation.Experiencer: Exactly, it wasn’t something I expected or even thought would happen, but it just felt... good, you know? Like it stayed with me longer than it should’ve.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s like it caught you off guard in the best way and left a mark you didn’t see coming.

conversation.Experiencer: My now husband proposed to me, and it felt like everything in that moment was perfectly aligned, as if it was meant to happen just the way it did.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a beautiful and special moment. What was it like when he proposed?

conversation.Experiencer: It was incredible—he had this calm confidence, like he knew exactly what he wanted to say and do, and all I could do was take it all in.

conversation.Responder: It must’ve been amazing to see him so sure and composed. How did you feel in that moment?

conversation.Experiencer: I felt completely swept up in it, like the whole world paused just for that moment to unfold exactly as it did.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so magical, like time stood still just for you two. What stands out the most when you think back on it?

conversation.Experiencer: A fight broke out in a bar I was in, and I had my girlfriend with me and couldn’t think how to get out. The whole place turned into chaos so fast—I kept looking for a way through, but it was like the walls were closing in with people everywhere.

conversation.Responder: That sounds intense and overwhelming, especially with everything happening so fast and no clear way out. What ended up happening?

conversation.Experiencer: I just tried to keep her close and stayed on edge, watching every movement around us. Eventually, the crowd shifted enough that we could push through toward the exit, but it felt like it took forever.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were really focused on protecting her and getting out of there. Must’ve been a huge relief when you finally made it to the exit.

conversation.Experiencer: Yeah, it was a relief, but even once we got outside, my head was still spinning. I kept replaying everything, trying to make sense of how it got so out of control so fast.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s hard to shake something like that, especially when it all escalated so suddenly. Sounds like it stuck with you even after you got out.

conversation.Experiencer: It was like everything slowed down, and I couldn’t figure out which way to move without making it worse.

conversation.Responder: That sounds intense, like you were caught in a moment where every choice felt risky. What happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: I just kept my hands locked on the wheel, watching every move that car made, hoping there’d be some opening to get out of its way.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were completely focused, just waiting for the right moment. Did you manage to find an opening?

conversation.Experiencer: There wasn’t really any clear opening—the car kept swerving all over, and I couldn’t predict where it would go next. It felt like I was stuck, just bracing for whatever might happen.

conversation.Responder: That must have been so nerve-wracking, just stuck there with no idea what was coming. How did it all end up?

conversation.Experiencer: I feel like I let her down when she probably needed someone to just check in, and I can't stop thinking about how easy it would’ve been to make the time.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it's really weighing on you how simple it could've been to reach out. What's been running through your mind about it?

conversation.Experiencer: I keep replaying those moments when I glanced at my phone, thinking I should do something, and then just… didn’t. It feels like I chose the wrong thing every time.

conversation.Responder: That sounds frustrating, like those missed chances are stuck in your head. What do you think kept you from acting in those moments?

conversation.Experiencer: I think I let myself get too wrapped up in my own schedule, convincing myself I’d do it later, but later never came. It’s like I made excuses instead of just prioritizing what actually mattered.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you're really seeing how those choices added up. What's standing out to you the most about that?

conversation.Experiencer: During a very heavy thunderstorm, I found myself watching the wind whip through the trees, wondering just how much stronger it might get.

conversation.Responder: That sounds intense—storms like that can really keep you on edge. What else did you notice while you were watching?

conversation.Experiencer: The rain was coming down so hard it blurred everything outside, and the gusts felt like they could push anything that wasn’t tied down.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like the storm was really wild. Did you see anything get caught in the wind?

conversation.Experiencer: A few branches broke off and tumbled down the street, but luckily nothing serious seemed to get swept away near me.

conversation.Responder: That’s a relief nothing major got taken by the wind. Did the storm last long?

conversation.Experiencer: I watched a sad film, and it left me sitting in silence for a while after it ended.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it really hit you hard. What was it about?

conversation.Experiencer: It was about people facing struggles that felt so raw and real, like you could almost feel their weight yourself.

conversation.Responder: That sounds intense—like it really pulled you into their world. What stuck with you the most?

conversation.Experiencer: The way it showed their quiet moments, the things unsaid, but still so loud—it just lingered with me.

conversation.Responder: Those quiet moments can carry so much meaning. Sounds like they really stayed with you.

conversation.Experiencer: I shouldn’t have said anything—looking back, it was such an avoidable slip, and I feel like I let everyone down.

conversation.Responder: That sounds really tough—you're feeling like that moment had a big impact. Want to talk more about what happened?

conversation.Experiencer: I guess I just didn’t pause long enough to think it through—what seemed harmless at the time ended up causing a lot more frustration than I expected.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it sounds like it escalated more than you thought it would. What kind of frustration came out of it?

conversation.Experiencer: They were upset because they’d put so much effort into keeping it a secret, and I pretty much unraveled that work in a split second. It was obvious how disappointed they were.

conversation.Responder: That must've been hard to see their reaction like that. How did things go after that moment?

conversation.Experiencer: Ugh, I barely had time to stop her—she just dove right in like it was the best find of the day.

conversation.Responder: Wow, sounds like she was really excited—what happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: Thankfully, she only gave it a quick lick and moved on like nothing happened, but I’m still cringing just thinking about it.

conversation.Responder: Oh man, that’s definitely one of those moments that sticks in your head—what was it she found?

conversation.Experiencer: I have no idea, honestly. Just some streaks on the ground that looked like something gross had been spilled earlier. I didn’t want to get too close to figure it out.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, I don’t blame you—better to just avoid it altogether. Did she seem totally unfazed after that?

conversation.Experiencer: One of my friends was mistreated by her husband, and I can’t stop thinking about how much she’s been through and how nobody stepped in to help her when she needed it.

conversation.Responder: That sounds really upsetting, especially knowing what she went through and feeling like no one was there for her. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: She told me about how things had been for her, and the more she talked, the more it became clear just how serious it all was. It’s hard to understand how people around her could have known and still done nothing.

conversation.Responder: It’s heartbreaking to hear how serious it was and to think about people just standing by. What else did she share with you?

conversation.Experiencer: She gave me specific examples of what he’s done, and honestly, it was hard to listen to—it’s like every detail made it worse. I kept wondering how she managed to cope with all of it on her own.

conversation.Responder: That sounds really heavy, especially hearing all those details and imagining what she went through alone. What stood out to you the most about what she said?

conversation.Experiencer:
I did not stay to do New Year with my parents.  

I keep wondering what it’ll feel like for them later tonight, setting up everything the way they always do, but with one less person there.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like it’s been on your mind a lot, thinking about how it might feel for them tonight.

conversation.Experiencer: Yeah, it crosses my mind every so often. I can picture them going through the usual motions, and I can’t help but think they’ll notice the gap I left.

conversation.Responder: You’re imagining it pretty vividly, like you can really see how it might stand out to them.

conversation.Experiencer: I guess I can’t help it—it’s so familiar, you know? The way they set the table, the little traditions we have… I just wonder if they’ll feel like something’s missing.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like those traditions mean a lot to you, and you’re really tuned into how things might feel different without you there.

conversation.Experiencer: I received recognition at work with a job role change and pay rise. It feels like all the effort I’ve been putting in has really paid off, and seeing everyone’s support today made it even more rewarding.

conversation.Responder: That's awesome! It must feel amazing to have your hard work recognized like that, especially with everyone cheering you on.

conversation.Experiencer: It really does. It’s one thing to put in the work, but having people notice and celebrate it with me makes it feel even more worthwhile.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that kind of support makes the whole experience so much more meaningful. Sounds like a really special moment for you.

conversation.Experiencer: It definitely was. It’s one of those moments where you can actually step back and see how far you’ve come—and it’s nice to know others see it too.

conversation.Responder: That’s such a great feeling, being able to reflect and see your progress like that. And having others recognize it just makes it even better.

conversation.Experiencer: I walked down a dark alley, and every step felt like it could lead to something I didn’t want to find.

conversation.Responder: That sounds tense—like the whole walk felt heavy with uncertainty. What happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: I kept moving, trying to stay alert, but the shadows kept shifting, and there was this sound—footsteps, maybe—but I couldn’t tell where it was coming from.

conversation.Responder: That sounds eerie, like the shadows and sounds were messing with your senses. Did you figure out where the footsteps were coming from?

conversation.Experiencer: No, I didn’t. It was impossible to tell—it felt like they were everywhere and nowhere at the same time.

conversation.Responder: That must’ve been so unsettling, like the whole place was closing in on you. What did you do next?

conversation.Experiencer: It feels like we let them down, even though we didn’t feel up to it—like we should’ve done more to make it work somehow.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you're carrying a lot of guilt about it. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: I guess we just sent a quick text and left it at that—didn’t really explain much or try to figure out another way to show up.

conversation.Responder: So it was kind of a short, unfinished interaction. What was going through your mind when you decided to leave it like that?

conversation.Experiencer: Honestly, I think we just felt so drained that even the smallest effort seemed like too much in the moment, even though we knew it wasn’t ideal.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were just completely out of energy and couldn’t push past that, even though part of you wished you could. How are you feeling about it now?

conversation.Experiencer: I wandered off from my parents in a busy department store as a child... I remember weaving through all those people, trying to spot them, but everything felt so chaotic, and I couldn't figure out where to look first.

conversation.Responder: That sounds overwhelming, especially with so much going on around you. What happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: I ended up stopping near this bright, colorful display—it caught my eye so much that I stayed there for a moment, even though I knew I should keep moving to find them.

conversation.Responder: That display must have been really distracting with all the colors. Did someone eventually help you or did you find your parents on your own?

conversation.Experiencer: Eventually, one of the store employees noticed me looking around and asked if I was lost. They helped me find my parents after that.

conversation.Responder: It’s good that the employee noticed and stepped in to help. How did you feel when you finally saw your parents again?

conversation.Experiencer: My oldest cat had to be put to sleep because an inoperable tumour was found. It all happened so quickly, and even though I knew there was nothing more that could be done, it doesn’t make the weight of it any lighter.

conversation.Responder: I'm really sorry you had to go through that. It sounds like it was such a heavy and sudden thing to face.

conversation.Experiencer: Thank you. It was one of those moments where you just have to keep going, even though every part of it feels like too much to bear.

conversation.Responder: That sounds incredibly hard. It’s one of those things that hits you deep while life keeps moving forward.

conversation.Experiencer: Yeah, it’s strange—everything feels quieter now, like there’s this space where they used to be, and I don’t quite know how to fill it yet.

conversation.Responder: I get what you mean—losing them leaves a noticeable gap, and it’s hard to figure out what to do with that emptiness.

conversation.Experiencer: I found the dead mouse behind the outside freezer. Honestly, it’s not the kind of thing you expect to deal with—you just sort of wish it wasn’t there at all.

conversation.Responder: Ugh, that’s gross. Definitely not something you want to stumble across.

conversation.Experiencer: Yeah, it wasn’t great. I checked to make sure it wasn’t still alive, but there wasn’t much else to do about it.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you handled it, even though it’s not the kind of thing anyone wants to deal with.

conversation.Experiencer: I just wish there was some way to avoid it altogether, but it’s not like I could’ve stopped it from happening.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, sometimes stuff like that feels completely out of your control. It’s frustrating.

conversation.Experiencer: I walk by homeless people on the street, and sometimes I can't help but wonder if doing nothing makes me part of the problem.

conversation.Experiencer: I guess I just keep asking myself what difference it would really make if I stopped—like, would it even help in the long run?

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you're really questioning how much impact you could have. What keeps that thought coming back for you?

conversation.Experiencer: I think it's because I know they're still out there, and I just walked past like it wasn’t my place to do anything. It feels... unresolved.

conversation.Responder: I get that—it’s like the situation stays with you even after you’ve walked away. What does "unresolved" feel like for you?

conversation.Experiencer: I still can’t help but think about how I could’ve moved quicker or done something differently to make it there in time.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it’s really weighing on you. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s just that everything felt so urgent in the moment—I dropped everything and rushed over, but no matter how hard I tried, I was just too late.

conversation.Responder: That sounds really tough, like you gave it everything but still couldn’t change how it turned out. Want to tell me more about it?

conversation.Experiencer: I keep replaying it in my head, you know? Every turn, every minute, like maybe if I’d left sooner or taken a different route, things would’ve been different.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it sounds like it’s hard to stop going over all the details. What sticks out the most when you think about it?

conversation.Experiencer: It's hard to wrap my head around how someone could justify walking away like that and leave their family to deal with the fallout.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that’s a lot to process—just leaving like that. Sounds like it’s been weighing heavily on you.

conversation.Experiencer: I keep thinking about the excuses they made, as if shifting blame could somehow make it all okay.

conversation.Responder: That sounds really frustrating, especially with the excuses. It’s tough when it feels like they’re avoiding accountability.

conversation.Experiencer: Exactly. It’s like they were more focused on defending their actions than even considering the damage they caused.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that really shows where their priorities were. It must feel so unfair seeing them ignore the impact they left behind.

conversation.Experiencer: It was one of those jobs where you don’t know whether to start cleaning or just torch the whole thing and walk away.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it was a total mess—one of those overwhelming situations. What ended up happening?

conversation.Experiencer: I tried to make a dent in it, but with just gloves and some spray, it felt like trying to empty a flood with a teaspoon.

conversation.Responder: That sounds frustrating, like it was never-ending. Did you keep going or just call it a day?

conversation.Experiencer: I kept at it because there wasn’t much choice, but honestly, it felt like I was barely making a difference.

conversation.Responder: That sounds exhausting, like you were stuck in a losing battle. How long did you end up working on it?

conversation.Experiencer: I was on a small plane and there was a lot of turbulence... Honestly, every time the plane dropped or jolted, my stomach felt like it was flipping inside out.

conversation.Responder: Wow, that sounds intense—those sudden drops must've been so jarring. What was going through your mind while it was happening?

conversation.Experiencer: I kept trying to convince myself it was just normal, but I couldn't stop imagining all the worst possibilities every time it shook again.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so overwhelming, like your brain just wouldn’t let it go. Did the turbulence last the whole flight?

conversation.Experiencer: Not the whole flight, but long enough that it felt like it might never stop. Every time it seemed to calm down, it would come back even worse.

conversation.Responder: That must've made it feel impossible to relax, especially with it coming back like that. How did you get through the rest of the flight?

conversation.Experiencer: I thought I had lost my wallet, and I’ve been going in circles trying to figure out what went wrong or where it could’ve ended up.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so frustrating, especially with all the backtracking you’ve been doing. Have you had any luck yet?

conversation.Experiencer: Not at all—every spot I’ve checked has turned up nothing, and I can’t shake the feeling that it’s just gone for good.

conversation.Responder: That’s such a sinking feeling, especially when you’ve tried everything. Do you remember the last time you had it?

conversation.Experiencer: The last time I know for sure was when I paid for lunch earlier, but after that, everything’s a blur—I keep second-guessing myself about what I did next.

conversation.Responder: That must be so stressful, not being sure what happened after lunch. Do you think retracing that part of your day might help?

conversation.Experiencer: I can't stand when people lie—especially when they double down on it over and over, like it’s some kind of game.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that’s so frustrating, especially when they won’t back down no matter what. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: It was just one of those moments where you’re sitting there, listening, and you know every word they’re saying is made up—but they just keep going, weaving this whole story like it’s going to hold together.

conversation.Responder: Ugh, that sounds so annoying, just watching them dig themselves in deeper. How did you even sit through that?

conversation.Experiencer: Honestly, I stayed quiet because it felt pointless to call them out—they weren’t going to admit anything, and the whole thing had this weird tension, like they were trying to prove something to everyone there.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so uncomfortable, like the whole room was caught in their performance. Did anyone else seem to notice?

conversation.Experiencer: I just can't believe how careless someone has to be to let something like this happen—it’s caused such a mess, and now I’m left dealing with the fallout.

conversation.Responder: That sounds incredibly frustrating, especially since you're the one stuck handling it. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: It was a tap left running upstairs, and by the time I found it, the bathroom was flooded and water was dripping into the room below. I spent hours trying to clean it up, but the damage is already done.

conversation.Responder: Wow, that sounds like such a nightmare. All that cleanup and damage must have been exhausting to deal with.

conversation.Experiencer: Exhausting doesn’t even cover it—every towel I used was soaked in minutes, and no matter how much I did, I couldn’t stop the water from seeping through. It was just nonstop.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so overwhelming, like no matter what you did, it just kept getting worse. How’s everything looking now?

conversation.Experiencer: I really wish I hadn’t done that—it’s been eating at me all day.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it’s been weighing on you a lot. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: I don’t know why I went through with it; it felt like such a split-second decision, but now it’s all I can think about.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it’s been hard to shake. What made it feel so impulsive?

conversation.Experiencer: I guess I just saw the chance and acted without really thinking it through, but now it’s like I can’t escape the regret.

conversation.Responder: That regret sounds really heavy. What’s been running through your mind about it?

conversation.Experiencer: That's the kind of failure that leaves you questioning how systems meant to protect the most vulnerable can fall apart so badly.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s frustrating when the systems that are supposed to help just completely let people down. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: It sounds like poor management within these centres is causing huge delays in processing evidence, and it's having devastating consequences for the kids involved.

conversation.Responder: That’s awful—those delays must be so hard on the kids who need help the most. What else have you heard about it?

conversation.Experiencer: The article shared some cases where the delays meant crucial evidence couldn’t be used, and it ended up affecting whether or not justice was served for the victims. It’s heartbreaking.

conversation.Responder: That’s so upsetting, especially knowing it’s costing victims their chance at justice. Did the article say anything about what’s being done to fix it?

conversation.Experiencer: It felt so special to see their face light up—it’s like all the time I spent planning and choosing was completely worth it.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a rewarding moment—it must’ve felt amazing to see all your effort pay off like that.

conversation.Experiencer: It really was—I couldn’t stop thinking about how perfectly it all came together, and how happy they seemed.

conversation.Responder: That’s such a great feeling, especially when everything just clicks like that and you can see how much it meant to them.

conversation.Experiencer: Exactly, it’s those little moments that remind you how meaningful it can be to put thought into something for someone else.

conversation.Responder: Totally, those kinds of moments really make it all feel worthwhile and stick with you in the best way.

conversation.Experiencer: I was given bad customer service by a store employee who barely acknowledged me and seemed more interested in rushing through everyone else.

conversation.Responder: That sounds frustrating, especially when you're just trying to get decent service. What happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: I ended up standing there for what felt like forever, trying to get their attention, but it was like I didn’t even exist to them.

conversation.Responder: That must’ve been so irritating, just being ignored like that. Did you ever get them to notice you?

conversation.Experiencer: Not really. Even when I tried to speak up, they just gave me these short, dismissive answers and moved on like I wasn’t worth their time.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so dismissive and rude. How did that leave you feeling afterward?

conversation.Experiencer: I couldn’t believe it—he just stayed up there like he had every right to be, and nothing I said made a difference.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like he was really stubborn about it. What happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: I kept watching, hoping he’d just get down and leave, but he didn’t budge no matter how many times I called out.

conversation.Responder: That must've been so frustrating to deal with. Did anything eventually get him to move?

conversation.Experiencer: No, he didn’t move at all. I felt stuck just standing there, waiting for something to happen.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so exhausting, just being stuck like that. How long did you end up waiting?

conversation.Experiencer: My bike was stolen after having left it unlocked for all of five minutes in my front garden. I can’t believe how quickly someone must’ve taken advantage of the situation—like they were just waiting for an opportunity.

conversation.Responder: That’s so frustrating—sounds like they wasted no time at all. Did you notice anything unusual before it happened?

conversation.Experiencer: No, nothing at all. It was just a normal day—people walking by, cars on the road—and then suddenly it’s gone, like it vanished into thin air.

conversation.Responder: That’s wild, especially since everything seemed so ordinary. Did you find out if anyone nearby saw anything?

conversation.Experiencer: I looked around and even checked with a couple of neighbors, but no one seems to have noticed anything. It’s like it happened right under everyone’s noses without a trace.

conversation.Responder: That’s so frustrating, especially with no leads from anyone nearby. Must feel like it just disappeared without a clue.

conversation.Experiencer: I was in a high place during a trip, and it felt like the world below was alive in a way I couldn’t quite grasp, no matter how hard I tried to follow it.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like a pretty surreal moment—like being part of something bigger but still separate. What else stood out to you while you were up there?

conversation.Experiencer: It was the way everything kept shifting—sounds, movements, patterns—like it was all connected somehow, but just out of reach whenever I tried to pin it down.

conversation.Responder: That constant shifting must have been mesmerizing, like trying to catch something that keeps slipping away. How did it feel to be caught up in that moment?

conversation.Experiencer: It was strange—almost like I couldn’t look away, even though I knew I could step back anytime. There was this pull to keep watching, even when it felt just a little... unsettling.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it had a magnetic kind of intensity, drawing you in even with that edge of unease. What kept you there, do you think?

conversation.Experiencer: I didn’t expect a simple song to reach so deeply into places I thought I’d tucked away.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like that song really hit something personal for you. What about it stood out?

conversation.Experiencer: It was the way the melody seemed to echo something familiar, like it was written to find me in that exact moment.

conversation.Responder: It’s amazing how music can feel so personal like that. What kind of feeling did it bring up for you?

conversation.Experiencer: It felt like a quiet pull toward memories I didn’t realize were still so close—heavy, but impossible to turn away from.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like it brought a lot to the surface for you. What kind of memories came up?

conversation.Experiencer: I called in sick for work one day when I wasn’t really sick. Honestly, it’s not something I feel great about—I could’ve handled things differently, but I made the choice I made.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you're feeling a bit conflicted about it. Want to share more about what led to that choice?

conversation.Experiencer: I guess I just felt like I needed a break, but instead of being upfront about it, I took the easier route. It wasn’t the most honest way to go about things, and that’s been sitting with me.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it sounds like that’s been weighing on you. What’s been on your mind about it since then?

conversation.Experiencer: I’ve been trying not to dwell on it too much, but it’s hard to ignore that I could’ve approached it differently. It’s not a choice I’m proud of, even though it felt simple in the moment.

conversation.Responder: I get that—it’s tough when something feels easy at the time but sticks with you afterward. What’s been standing out to you most about it?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s like everything else fades away, and all you can do is marvel at how something so small can feel so immense in your hands.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it really took your breath away—what was it?

conversation.Experiencer: It was holding my baby for the first time, watching those tiny fingers grip mine like they already knew me somehow.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so powerful, like such a deep connection in that moment. What was going through your mind?

conversation.Experiencer: I kept wondering who they’ll become, what their little expressions were trying to tell me—every movement felt like a glimpse of something bigger I couldn’t fully grasp yet.

conversation.Responder: That sounds so overwhelming, like every little detail held so much meaning. What did you notice the most?

conversation.Experiencer: I listened to the national news, and it’s clear that the choices made earlier by those in charge left little room for anything to turn out differently.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it does feel like things were set in motion a while back. What stood out to you the most from the news?

conversation.Experiencer: The way they outlined the decisions leading up to this—it just reinforced how much was already locked in before anyone could really react.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like they laid it out pretty clearly. What part of it hit you the hardest?

conversation.Experiencer: Probably seeing how avoidable some of it might have been if different choices were made earlier—it’s frustrating to think about.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that kind of "what if" feeling can really stick with you. What else is on your mind about it?

conversation.Experiencer: I made a mistake at work, and somebody else was blamed. I sent a follow-up to straighten things out, but honestly, I should’ve been clearer from the start.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you're feeling bad about how it played out, especially since it caused confusion for someone else. What happened after you sent the follow-up?

conversation.Experiencer: I explained the correct steps and made sure everyone was on the same page moving forward. It wasn’t a big email, but I wanted to make sure things didn’t snowball further.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you handled it pretty directly and made sure to clear things up before it got worse. How did people respond to the email?

conversation.Experiencer: People seemed fine with it—nobody made a big deal out of it, so I think it helped settle things. I just hope it didn’t leave any lasting frustration behind.

conversation.Responder: It’s good that things seemed to smooth over and no one reacted strongly. Do you feel like it’s still on your mind, though?

conversation.Experiencer: I was walking in a large city at night on my own, and every little noise or shadow made me feel like I had to keep looking over my shoulder.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it was a tense walk, with everything around you keeping you on edge. What was it like trying to stay alert the whole time?

conversation.Experiencer: It felt like I couldn’t let my guard down for even a second—every step had to be careful, and every sound made me second-guess what might be around me.

conversation.Responder: That sounds exhausting, like you were constantly scanning for something to happen. What stood out to you the most during all of that?

conversation.Experiencer: The way the shadows seemed to shift and the noises came out of nowhere—it was like the streets were alive with things I couldn’t see.

conversation.Responder: That’s such an eerie image, like the whole city was moving around you in ways you couldn’t predict. How did it feel being surrounded by that?

conversation.Experiencer: I took my weekly bin in after bin collection to find it crawling with maggots. Honestly, I just stood there for a second, trying to decide if I could even deal with it right then or if it was something I’d rather put off.

conversation.Responder: Ugh, that sounds disgusting. Did you end up dealing with it right away or leave it for later?

conversation.Experiencer: I couldn’t just leave it like that—it was too nasty to ignore. I grabbed some gloves and supplies and got to work, though it wasn’t exactly how I planned to spend my time.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you powered through it even though it was gross. How long did it take you to clean it up?

conversation.Experiencer: It felt like forever, but maybe 20 minutes or so? I had to scrub every corner to make sure there wasn’t a single trace left.

conversation.Responder: That sounds exhausting, but at least you got it done. Was the smell as bad as the cleanup?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s frustrating when someone shuts down an idea without even giving it proper consideration.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that can be really annoying, especially when you feel like they didn’t even take the time to hear you out.

conversation.Experiencer: Exactly. It’s not like I was trying to complicate things—I was offering a way to make the process smoother for everyone.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were just trying to help, and they didn’t see it that way.

conversation.Experiencer: I know, and it’s hard not to feel like the effort was wasted when they’re so set in their ways.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s frustrating when it feels like your input doesn’t even get a chance to matter.

conversation.Experiencer: I went on holiday and left our cat behind, and honestly, I can’t stop wondering if I made a terrible mistake.

conversation.Responder: It sounds like you're really worried about leaving your cat behind. What’s been on your mind about it?

conversation.Experiencer: I keep thinking about whether she’s okay or if something’s gone wrong, and it’s hard to shake the thought that I could’ve done more before I left.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you’re stuck replaying it over and over. What do you think you could’ve done differently?

conversation.Experiencer: I had plenty of chances to sort something out, like asking someone to check on her or setting up proper care, but I just… didn’t follow through.

conversation.Responder: It seems like you’re feeling pretty frustrated with yourself about that. What’s making it stick with you so much?

conversation.Experiencer: I went into my back garden and found someone’s cat had been pooping on my lawn, and I had to pick up over 10 poops. Honestly, I don’t know what’s worse—the smell, the sheer amount of it, or the fact that I can’t do a thing to stop it from happening again.

conversation.Responder: Ugh, that sounds so gross and frustrating—dealing with all that and knowing it could just keep happening. What did you do after cleaning it up?

conversation.Experiencer: I just double-checked the lawn to make sure I hadn’t missed anything, then bagged it all up and tossed it out. After that, I came back inside and washed my hands about five times. I still feel like the smell is stuck in my nose.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a hassle, and I get why the smell would still linger—it’s hard to shake off something that gross. Did it take long to get it all cleaned up?

conversation.Experiencer: Yeah, it took a while. I had to keep stopping to make sure I didn’t overlook anything, and bending down over and over wasn’t exactly fun. The whole thing just felt like it dragged on forever.

conversation.Responder: That does sound like it turned into such a tedious, never-ending task. Did it leave you feeling completely drained afterward?

conversation.Experiencer: I really should have managed my time better—it's frustrating knowing this could've been avoided.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s tough when you can see how it could’ve gone differently. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: I underestimated how long it would take to get there, and then the traffic just made it worse—it wasn’t fair to leave them waiting like that.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like the timing just got away from you, especially with the traffic. How did they react?

conversation.Experiencer: They were understanding, thankfully, but I know it wasn’t ideal for them. I hate that I put them in that position.

conversation.Responder: It’s good they were understanding, but I get why you’d still feel bad about it. What’s on your mind now?

conversation.Experiencer: My ex-husband managed to access my bank account and withdrew money from it to pay into his own account. I’ve been digging through every detail to piece together how he pulled this off, and it’s exhausting trying to untangle the mess he’s left me with.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like a complete nightmare to deal with. How did you find out he got into your account?

conversation.Experiencer: I noticed some strange activity while checking my account, and when I dug deeper, the transaction history made it pretty clear it was him. The timestamps and account details left no room for doubt.

conversation.Responder: Wow, that must have been such a shock to see. What did you do once you realized it was him?

conversation.Experiencer: I spent hours gathering all the evidence I could—screenshots, transaction logs, everything—and then I contacted customer service to start the process of disputing it. It was such a draining process, but I couldn’t just let it slide.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a hassle, but it makes sense you’d want to document everything. How’s the dispute process going so far?

conversation.Experiencer: I had my exam results and I had passed my Staff Nurse training. It feels surreal, almost like I need to keep looking at the confirmation just to make it sink in.

conversation.Responder: That's amazing, congrats! It makes sense you'd keep checking—it’s a huge deal.

conversation.Experiencer: Thank you! Honestly, seeing it written out like that feels like the perfect closure to all those nights spent studying—it was all worth it.

conversation.Responder: Absolutely, it sounds like all that hard work paid off big time. Must feel so satisfying to see it all come together like this.

conversation.Experiencer: It really does. Knowing that every bit of effort went into something meaningful—it’s just the best feeling.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that sense of accomplishment is unbeatable. You earned every bit of it!

conversation.Experiencer: I failed my ninth year at high school. I keep thinking about the report and all the chances I had to do better—like when people offered to help or shared advice—and I just didn’t take them seriously enough.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like those missed chances are really sticking with you. What’s been going through your mind about it all?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s hard not to replay it—seeing how I let things pile up and didn’t put the effort in when it mattered. It’s like every grade on that report is a reminder of what I could’ve done differently.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it sounds like those grades feel like a constant reminder of what happened. What else has been coming up for you when you think about it?

conversation.Experiencer: I guess it’s just the realization that I had the tools to succeed—people offering help, enough time to study—and I chose not to use them. It’s hard to sit with knowing it was all avoidable.

conversation.Responder: That realization sounds really heavy, especially knowing it could've gone differently. How are you feeling as you’re sitting with all of this?

conversation.Experiencer: It happened so fast—I barely had time to react, and it’s been stuck in my head ever since.

conversation.Responder: That sounds overwhelming—like it’s hard to shake off. What happened?

conversation.Experiencer: I was cycling along, keeping to my lane, and suddenly this car just shot out at the junction without even slowing down.

conversation.Responder: Wow, that must’ve been so jarring—did you manage to avoid it?

conversation.Experiencer: Yeah, I swerved just enough, but it was way too close for comfort. I don’t even think they noticed me.

conversation.Responder: That sounds terrifying—it’s like they didn’t even care. Were you okay afterward?

conversation.Experiencer: I probably pushed it more than I should have, but with the rain and the time ticking down, it felt like there wasn’t much choice.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were under a lot of pressure with the time and weather working against you. What happened next?

conversation.Experiencer: The roads were so slick that every turn felt like a gamble, and I had to constantly adjust just to keep things steady—it was exhausting trying to balance speed and control.

conversation.Responder: That sounds intense, having to fight for control the whole time. How did it all play out?

conversation.Experiencer: I managed to get us there, but the whole way, I couldn’t stop thinking about how close everything felt to going wrong—it’s not a drive I’d want to repeat.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like a nerve-wracking trip. Must’ve been a relief to finally get there.

conversation.Experiencer: I realised that there was an outbreak of head lice in my daughter's class, and honestly, it's frustrating how something like this could spread so far before anyone acted on it properly.

conversation.Responder: That does sound really frustrating, especially when it feels like it could’ve been handled sooner. How are you dealing with it now?

conversation.Experiencer: I’ve done everything I can at home—got the treatments, followed all the steps—but it’s hard to feel like it’ll make much difference when not everyone seems to be catching it early enough.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you’ve been really thorough, but it must be discouraging when others aren’t as quick to act. How’s your daughter handling all this?

conversation.Experiencer: She’s doing fine, thankfully, but I hate that she has to deal with this at all. It’s not fair to the kids when something like this spreads because people don’t catch it in time.

conversation.Responder: It really isn’t fair, especially when it’s out of her control. At least she’s doing okay—how are you feeling about everything?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s hard to wrap my head around how someone could be so reckless with another person’s trust and well-being.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s really frustrating when someone just disregards something so important like that.

conversation.Experiencer: Exactly. It’s like they didn’t even stop to think about the consequences or how their choices could affect me.

conversation.Responder: That’s the worst—when it feels like they didn’t even consider how much their actions would hurt you.

conversation.Experiencer: It makes me wonder if they even cared at all or if they just chose to ignore the damage they were causing.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s hard not to question if they even cared when their actions seemed so thoughtless.

conversation.Experiencer: I guess I just did what made things easier for everyone, even if it doesn’t sit right with me.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like a tough spot to be in. What’s been sitting wrong with you about it?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s not like I wanted to be dishonest, but the whole thing felt like it had to be handled this way to avoid any friction.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were stuck between being honest and keeping the peace. What was it about the situation that made it feel like there wasn’t another option?

conversation.Experiencer: The way they made it clear they didn’t want that person involved—it left no room to do anything differently without causing problems.

conversation.Responder: That must have put you in a tricky spot, feeling like your hands were tied. What happened after you went along with it?

conversation.Experiencer: Someone stabbed a man on a train, and I just sat there, frozen, watching it all happen like I was stuck in a nightmare.

conversation.Responder: That sounds terrifying, just being stuck there watching it unfold. What happened after?

conversation.Experiencer: After it happened, everything was chaos—people shouting, some trying to move, but it felt like we were all trapped in this horrible moment with nowhere to go.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like it was overwhelming, with everyone caught up in the chaos. What did you do next?

conversation.Experiencer: I didn’t do much of anything—I couldn’t. It felt like my body just refused to move, like all I could do was sit there and take it all in, even though I didn’t want to.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were completely frozen in the moment, just stuck there while everything was happening around you. What happened after that?

conversation.Experiencer: I over-ate on snacks, and honestly, I don't even want to think about how much.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that happens sometimes. What kind of snacks were you into?

conversation.Experiencer: Just the usual stuff—nothing special. I guess I went through more than I planned, though.

conversation.Responder: Gotcha, easy to lose track with that kind of stuff. Were you just chilling or doing something while snacking?

conversation.Experiencer: I was just sitting around, not really doing much. It’s like I set myself up for it, honestly.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, just sitting around can make it easy to keep grabbing snacks. What were you up to while hanging out?

conversation.Experiencer: I found out that a good friend of mine had money stolen by a carer. It's infuriating how someone trusted could take advantage like that, and it just eats at me knowing it might have been avoided with a bit more caution.

conversation.Responder: That’s really upsetting, especially when it’s someone who’s supposed to be trusted. It makes sense why that would bother you so much.

conversation.Experiencer: It’s just hard to wrap my head around how someone could do that, knowing the harm they’re causing. It feels so calculated, like they knew exactly what they were doing and didn’t care about the consequences for my friend.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s awful to think about how deliberate and cold that kind of behavior is. It must be really frustrating to see your friend put through that.

conversation.Experiencer: It is. They didn’t deserve this, and it makes me sick to think about how much trust they placed in someone who just threw it away for their own gain.

conversation.Responder: That’s heartbreaking, especially knowing how much trust was betrayed. It’s hard to see someone you care about go through something like this.

conversation.Experiencer: I was on a ferry in a storm and lots of people were vomiting—honestly, it felt like everything around me was conspiring to make it unbearable, and there was no escape from it.

conversation.Responder: That sounds awful—being stuck there with all that going on must’ve been overwhelming. What was going through your mind during all of that?

conversation.Experiencer: I kept thinking about how I couldn’t get away from it—the noise, the smell, the chaos—it was like every sense was under attack, and there was no way to block any of it out.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a sensory overload, like there was no way to catch a break from it all. How long were you stuck in that situation?

conversation.Experiencer: It felt like forever, but I’m not even sure how long it actually was—time kind of stopped mattering when every second was just trying not to lose my footing or my stomach.

conversation.Responder: That must’ve made it feel even more endless, just trying to get through each moment like that. What finally happened when it was over?

conversation.Experiencer: I was on a flight as I am not the most comfortable with flying, and every little bump had me on edge, hoping for some reassurance that never came.

conversation.Responder: That sounds really tense, being on edge like that the whole flight. What was the hardest part for you?

conversation.Experiencer: Probably not knowing what was going on. I kept looking around for some kind of update or sign that everything was fine, but there was just nothing to hold onto.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that feeling of not knowing can make it so much worse. Did it stay like that the whole time?

conversation.Experiencer: It wasn’t constant, but every time the plane shook even a little, it pulled me right back into it. I just couldn’t fully settle or feel at ease.

conversation.Responder: That stop-and-start tension sounds exhausting. Did anything at all help you get through it?

conversation.Experiencer: I attended the New Year Organ Recital at Norwich Cathedral and listened to a varied selection of seasonal music. The way the organist brought each piece to life was mesmerizing—watching their hands and hearing the music fill the cathedral felt almost like stepping into another world.

conversation.Responder: That sounds incredible—like the whole experience was almost transporting with the music and the atmosphere of the cathedral. What stood out to you the most?

conversation.Experiencer: The way the acoustics carried every note so clearly was unforgettable—it felt like the music was weaving through the air, connecting everything around me. And seeing the organist’s precision up close added a whole new layer to it.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like the combination of the sound and the skill of the organist made it really immersive. Did it feel like certain pieces stood out more to you?

conversation.Experiencer: Absolutely—there was one intricate piece where the organist’s hands were moving so rapidly across the keys, it was almost hypnotic. The way they introduced each piece beforehand made it even more meaningful, like you could follow the story behind the music.

conversation.Responder: That must’ve made it feel so much more personal, knowing the story behind the music while watching such skill in action. Did that piece end up being your favorite?

conversation.Experiencer: I read that a 97-year-old man had been beaten up and robbed—it's horrifying to think how someone could do such a thing, especially to someone so vulnerable.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that's awful. It’s hard to wrap your head around how anyone could do something like that.

conversation.Experiencer: It really makes you question what kind of person could look at an elderly man and see an easy target instead of someone who deserves care and respect.

conversation.Responder: Totally, it’s just so messed up—he should’ve been treated with dignity, not attacked like that.

conversation.Experiencer: And the area where it happened—those pictures made it clear how unsafe it is. It’s like the whole setup just invited something terrible to happen.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, the surroundings seemed really sketchy. It’s like the place itself added to the risk.

conversation.Experiencer: I ate a sausage that was horrible. I don’t know what they did to it, but the taste was all wrong—sour, mushy, and just...off.

conversation.Responder: Ugh, that sounds awful. Was it from a restaurant or something you made at home?

conversation.Experiencer: It was from the market; I picked it out myself. I really thought it’d be decent, but clearly, I misjudged.

conversation.Responder: That’s so frustrating, especially when you were expecting it to be good. Did it ruin the whole meal for you?

conversation.Experiencer: Not entirely, thankfully. I stopped after the first bite, so I didn’t let it drag everything down.

conversation.Responder: Smart move stopping after the first bite. At least the rest of the meal wasn’t a total loss!

conversation.Experiencer: It’s unsettling how often drivers just keep going, even when people are clearly stepping out onto the crossing.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s frustrating when they don’t stop—it feels like they’re just ignoring basic rules.

conversation.Experiencer: It makes you wonder what’s going through their minds—or if they’re even paying attention at all.

conversation.Responder: Totally, it’s like you can’t tell if they’re distracted or just don’t care.

conversation.Experiencer: Either way, it’s dangerous. Someone’s going to get hurt if this keeps happening.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it’s scary how easily it could lead to an accident.

conversation.Experiencer: I’ve been trying to get ahead of things, but it’s exhausting not knowing if all this effort will even be enough.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that sounds really draining—putting in so much and still feeling unsure about the outcome. What’s been weighing on you the most?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s the not knowing—whether shelves will actually empty out or if I’m overpreparing. Every time I think I’ve got a handle on it, something in the news makes me question everything again.

conversation.Responder: That back-and-forth sounds really stressful, especially with everything feeling so uncertain. What did you see in the news that made you second-guess?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s all the conflicting statements—one moment they’re saying progress is being made, and the next it’s all falling apart. It feels impossible to tell what’s real or how bad things could get.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that back-and-forth must make it so much harder to feel grounded. It sounds like it’s been tough to sort through all of that noise.

conversation.Experiencer: I was cleaning out the shower drain and discovered that hair conditioner could leave a slimy mess. Honestly, I had no idea it could build up like that—it was such a nightmare trying to get it all out, especially with how awful the smell was down there.

conversation.Responder: Ugh, that sounds so gross! I bet dealing with the smell made it even worse.

conversation.Experiencer: It really did—every time I thought I was making progress, the stink would hit me again, and it just felt like it was never-ending.

conversation.Responder: That sounds brutal, like it just wouldn't let up no matter what you did. Did you finally manage to get it all cleaned out?

conversation.Experiencer: I did, but it took way longer than I thought it would. I had to switch tools halfway through because the first ones just weren’t cutting it—it was like fighting against glue in the worst possible spot.

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a frustrating process, especially having to switch tools mid-job. At least it’s finally done now!

conversation.Experiencer: It’s infuriating how they just push ahead, completely ignoring the clear evidence and the voices that should matter.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, that sounds really frustrating. What happened exactly?

conversation.Experiencer: They had every opportunity to consider alternatives that were backed by solid data, but instead, they chose to shut out public input and stick to their own agenda without a second thought.

conversation.Responder: That must feel so dismissive, especially when the data and input were there. What did their decision end up looking like?

conversation.Experiencer: They moved forward with their plans as if the public’s perspective didn’t exist—completely disregarding what could have been a more balanced or effective approach.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like they just bulldozed ahead without even acknowledging other viewpoints. How’s that sitting with you now?

conversation.Experiencer: I was climbing a tall tower, and every move felt like solving a puzzle where one wrong piece could send it all crumbling.

conversation.Responder: That sounds intense, like every step was high stakes. What was going through your mind while you were figuring it all out?

conversation.Experiencer: I couldn’t think too far ahead—just focused on the next grip, the next foothold, making sure nothing slipped out of place.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were completely in the moment, just trying to keep everything steady. What was it like when you finally reached the top?

conversation.Responder: That sounds exhausting, like it’s just getting harder as you go. How are you holding up with all of that?

conversation.Experiencer: I lied about my employment experience on a job application letter. I keep thinking, maybe I could’ve written something better without twisting the truth, but it felt like I had no other option at the time.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you were stuck in a tough spot and felt pressured to do that. What’s been on your mind about it since?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s been gnawing at me, honestly. I know I had the choice to approach it differently, but I rushed through and didn’t give myself room to rethink it properly.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like it’s really been weighing on you. Do you feel like it’s still affecting how you’re thinking about the situation now?

conversation.Experiencer: It is, yeah. I keep wondering if I could’ve handled it better, like if being upfront would’ve still given me a shot. It’s hard not to second-guess it now.

conversation.Responder: I get that—it’s tough not to replay it and wonder about the “what-ifs.” Does it feel like this is something you’re carrying around a lot lately?

conversation.Experiencer: I stayed alone at home, and every little sound felt like it could be something more.

conversation.Responder: That sounds tense—like every noise kept you on edge. What was it like sitting with that feeling?

conversation.Experiencer: It was hard to shake off, like my mind wouldn’t let me settle—every creak or flicker pulled me back to wondering what might happen next.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like your mind was stuck in overdrive, scanning for something to react to. How long did that feeling stick around?

conversation.Experiencer: It stayed with me the whole time, like I couldn’t switch it off—I kept waiting for something to confirm whether it was just the storm or something else.

conversation.Responder: That sounds exhausting, being caught in that kind of waiting. Did the storm make it even harder to relax?

conversation.Experiencer: I still remember gripping onto the harness so tightly, feeling every jolt and twist like it was completely out of my hands.

conversation.Responder: Sounds like that moment really stuck with you—those jolts must have felt intense. What else do you remember about it?

conversation.Experiencer: I remember staring at the track ahead, trying to make sense of what was coming, but most of it was hidden, and that made it worse somehow.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, not knowing what’s coming can make it feel so much more overwhelming. What was going through your mind as you stared at the track?

conversation.Responder: That sounds like such a tense buildup, just sitting there with all those thoughts. How did it feel once it actually started?

conversation.Experiencer: I haven't been as successful in my life as friends have been, and sometimes I wonder how much of a difference it would have made if I'd had access to the same kind of support or connections they did.

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it's hard not to think about how things might have been different with more support or connections. What’s been on your mind about it lately?

conversation.Responder: Sounds like you're really reflecting on those moments and wondering how much they mattered in the long run. What stands out to you most when you think back on that?

conversation.Responder: Yeah, it sounds like seeing how things lined up for them really sticks with you. Do you feel like that realization has changed how you look at those times now?

conversation.Experiencer: It’s infuriating to think that so many families are being dragged through unnecessary financial pain just because one man refuses to back down from such a misguided priority.

conversation.Responder: Totally, it does seem reckless when better choices are right there and just get tossed aside. What else are you thinking about this?
